current_focus: Treat K=128 only as the initial search anchor, not as a success criterion. The research objective is to find configurations whose FVU is dramatically better than the current K=128 baseline, with a target on the order of halving that baseline FVU before rewarding smaller K or lower cost.
architecture_findings: round 1: A JumpReLU encoder with learnable per-latent thresholds can improve the K=128 quality anchor versus plain TopK by suppressing weak activations before top-k selection, improving feature selectivity and lowering FVU without changing the execution layer. -> crash (fvu=None, observed_fvu=None, k=None, health=crash, termination=identical_to_baseline)

round 2: The new JumpReLU family deserves its first proxy measurement at the K=128 quality anchor; learnable thresholds with smooth gating may suppress weak activations and beat plain TopK FVU before any cost-oriented moves. -> keep (fvu=0.1887873373925686, observed_fvu=0.184100, k=128, health=normal, termination=completed)

round 7: A gated SAE that decouples latent support selection from reconstruction magnitude can beat the current JumpReLU anchor at K=128 by learning a separate sigmoid routing branch while staying initialization-close to plain ReLU top-k. -> crash (fvu=None, observed_fvu=None, k=None, health=crash, termination=identical_to_baseline)

round 26: Recent gated LR sweeps regressed, which suggests the quality ceiling is now architectural rather than a simple optimizer bracket. A new routed SAE family that separates top-k selection scores from decoded magnitudes, while warm-starting the support branch from the magnitude encoder, may preserve useful medium-strength features that plain gated currently suppresses too early and thus reduce FVU at K=128. -> crash (fvu=None, observed_fvu=None, k=None, health=crash, termination=identical_to_baseline)

round 30: The routed family did not get a fair read because its initial support selection was too close to baseline top-k and tripped the identical-to-baseline guard. Making routed support depend on an independently initialized router with a direct, non-monotone routing bonus should produce a genuine architecture signal in proxy and reveal whether separate support selection can outperform the stalled gated search. -> crash (fvu=None, observed_fvu=None, k=None, health=crash, termination=identical_to_baseline)

round 32: Severe stagnation and two non-competitive K=128 architecture probes suggest the current global-ranking support mechanisms are locally exhausted. A new `group_topk` prototype that enforces one local winner per small latent group before the global top-k may reduce redundant feature selection and improve reconstruction quality or cost under the same validated training regime. -> crash (fvu=None, observed_fvu=None, k=None, health=crash, termination=invalid_env_overrides)

round 43: `topk` has now repeated the same K=128 proxy result under the stronger regime and remains far from the gated frontier, so more selector-only variants are unlikely to close the quality gap. The next informative move is to prototype a more expressive encoder family inside `sparsify/`: a factorized top-k SAE that inserts a learned intermediate bottleneck and nonlinearity before latent selection. This changes the encoder hypothesis, not the training recipe, and tests whether extra pre-selection mixing can recover substantially lower FVU without committing to full gating complexity. -> crash (fvu=None, observed_fvu=None, k=None, health=crash, termination=sanity_failed)
performance_findings: round 3: suspected performance regression for jumprelu (tps=16601.650675, baseline_ratio=0.200000, termination=throughput_too_low)

round 39: `group_topk` still has no valid quality signal because its first actual proxy run timed out at the first step, which is stronger evidence of an implementation bottleneck than of a bad architecture. The next coherent move is a performance-focused code pass that makes the `group_topk` encoder path more execution-friendly so the family can receive one clean proxy measurement at K=128 under the strongest current regime. -> crash (fvu=None, observed_fvu=None, k=None, health=crash, termination=sanity_failed)

round 41: The recent `first_step_timeout` crashes are not specific to `group_topk`: the gated AUXK=0 recovery run also advanced through the first few microbatches and then stalled at the first optimizer step. That points to a shared training-path bottleneck rather than an architecture verdict. Replacing CUDA's per-step fired-latent `cat`+advanced-index update with an incremental boolean fired mask in `sparsify/trainer.py` should remove that first-step spike and let the unresolved `group_topk` K=128 proxy run complete under the current strongest regime. -> crash (fvu=None, observed_fvu=None, k=None, health=crash, termination=sanity_failed)

round 46: The obvious gated one-parameter sweep is now exhausted after clean regressions on both lower LR and lower expansion, so the next least-bad informative move is to unblock the incubating `factorized_topk` family rather than introduce another fresh direction. Its only evidence so far is a `sanity_failed` prototype, which should be treated as an architecture-compatibility issue, not a quality result. A narrow code fix inside `sparsify/` to make `factorized_topk` forward/backward compatible with the runtime should recover one valid proxy measurement at K=128 under the existing strongest regime. -> crash (fvu=None, observed_fvu=None, k=None, health=crash, termination=sanity_failed)

round 47: The straightforward gated hyperparameter sweep has now produced only clean regressions, so the next informative move is to recover the blocked incubating family rather than spend another round on weak local tuning. `factorized_topk` still has no usable quality signal because its only attempt failed at sanity. A narrow architecture-compatibility fix inside `sparsify/` should let the same K=128 proxy prototype run and finally reveal whether extra encoder mixing can improve over plain top-k. -> crash (fvu=None, observed_fvu=, k=None, health=normal, termination=completed)

round 49: `factorized_topk` has now failed for a concrete architecture-registration reason rather than an ambiguous quality or runtime signal: the execution layer rejects the architecture name in `SparseCoderConfig` before training begins. The next coherent move is a narrow code-fix round that wires `factorized_topk` into the existing config/class registry and forward path so the prototype can finally receive one real K=128 proxy measurement under the strongest current regime. -> crash (fvu=None, observed_fvu=, k=None, health=crash, termination=completed)

failure_patterns.pattern: A JumpReLU encoder with learnable per-latent thresholds can improve the K=128 quality anchor versus plain TopK by suppressing weak activations before top-k selection, improving feature selectivity and lowering FVU without changing the execution layer.

JumpReLU can reduce FVU further at K=128 by making its gating sharper and more selective; increasing the initial threshold while narrowing the bandwidth should suppress weak activations more aggressively and improve reconstruction quality.

A gated SAE that decouples latent support selection from reconstruction magnitude can beat the current JumpReLU anchor at K=128 by learning a separate sigmoid routing branch while staying initialization-close to plain ReLU top-k.

The current JumpReLU search direction appears locally exhausted, and a gated SAE that separates support selection from reconstruction magnitude may unlock a lower-FVU regime at K=128 by learning support with an explicit sigmoid routing branch while keeping the strong baseline optimization settings.

JumpReLU tuning appears fundamentally stalled, so the next credible move is to test the gated SAE family at K=128, where a separate sigmoid routing branch may learn cleaner support patterns than JumpReLU and reduce FVU under the same strong optimization settings.

Recent gated LR sweeps regressed, which suggests the quality ceiling is now architectural rather than a simple optimizer bracket. A new routed SAE family that separates top-k selection scores from decoded magnitudes, while warm-starting the support branch from the magnitude encoder, may preserve useful medium-strength features that plain gated currently suppresses too early and thus reduce FVU at K=128.

The routed family did not get a fair read because its initial support selection was too close to baseline top-k and tripped the identical-to-baseline guard. Making routed support depend on an independently initialized router with a direct, non-monotone routing bonus should produce a genuine architecture signal in proxy and reveal whether separate support selection can outperform the stalled gated search.

Severe stagnation and two non-competitive K=128 architecture probes suggest the current global-ranking support mechanisms are locally exhausted. A new `group_topk` prototype that enforces one local winner per small latent group before the global top-k may reduce redundant feature selection and improve reconstruction quality or cost under the same validated training regime.

After nine non-improving rounds and non-competitive K=128 results from topk and routed, the current global support-ranking direction looks structurally weak. The newly added `group_topk` family is the next fundamentally different architecture probe because it imposes local winner-take-all competition before global selection, which may improve feature diversity and reduce redundant support allocation under the same validated training regime.

The last round showed the gated low-K capacity cut was a real quality regression, and the search has now gone several rounds without advancing an incubating alternative family. The least-bad informative move is to get a real proxy read on the already-implemented `group_topk` family at K=128 under the strongest current training regime, removing the unsupported `GROUP_TOPK_SIZE` override that caused the earlier policy crashes. This will finally test whether local winner-take-all competition can beat other non-gated support selectors on anchor-quality rather than being discarded for an execution-layer mismatch.

`group_topk` still has no valid quality signal because its first actual proxy run timed out at the first step, which is stronger evidence of an implementation bottleneck than of a bad architecture. The next coherent move is a performance-focused code pass that makes the `group_topk` encoder path more execution-friendly so the family can receive one clean proxy measurement at K=128 under the strongest current regime.

In crash recovery mode, the safest informative move is to return to the current best working gated K=128 configuration and make the smallest remaining stabilization adjustment. Setting AUXK_ALPHA from 0.01 to 0 tests whether the new gated anchor is still slightly over-regularized while keeping architecture, K, expansion factor, optimizer, and learning rate fixed.

The recent `first_step_timeout` crashes are not specific to `group_topk`: the gated AUXK=0 recovery run also advanced through the first few microbatches and then stalled at the first optimizer step. That points to a shared training-path bottleneck rather than an architecture verdict. Replacing CUDA's per-step fired-latent `cat`+advanced-index update with an incremental boolean fired mask in `sparsify/trainer.py` should remove that first-step spike and let the unresolved `group_topk` K=128 proxy run complete under the current strongest regime.

`topk` has now repeated the same K=128 proxy result under the stronger regime and remains far from the gated frontier, so more selector-only variants are unlikely to close the quality gap. The next informative move is to prototype a more expressive encoder family inside `sparsify/`: a factorized top-k SAE that inserts a learned intermediate bottleneck and nonlinearity before latent selection. This changes the encoder hypothesis, not the training recipe, and tests whether extra pre-selection mixing can recover substantially lower FVU without committing to full gating complexity.

The obvious gated one-parameter sweep is now exhausted after clean regressions on both lower LR and lower expansion, so the next least-bad informative move is to unblock the incubating `factorized_topk` family rather than introduce another fresh direction. Its only evidence so far is a `sanity_failed` prototype, which should be treated as an architecture-compatibility issue, not a quality result. A narrow code fix inside `sparsify/` to make `factorized_topk` forward/backward compatible with the runtime should recover one valid proxy measurement at K=128 under the existing strongest regime.

The straightforward gated hyperparameter sweep has now produced only clean regressions, so the next informative move is to recover the blocked incubating family rather than spend another round on weak local tuning. `factorized_topk` still has no usable quality signal because its only attempt failed at sanity. A narrow architecture-compatibility fix inside `sparsify/` should let the same K=128 proxy prototype run and finally reveal whether extra encoder mixing can improve over plain top-k.

`factorized_topk` still lacks a usable proxy quality signal, and the most recent failure evidence points to the sanity invocation itself rather than a meaningful architecture outcome. The least-bad next move is to rerun the same `factorized_topk` K=128 proxy comparison without further code churn so the family can receive one clean measurement under the current strongest regime.

`factorized_topk` has now failed for a concrete architecture-registration reason rather than an ambiguous quality or runtime signal: the execution layer rejects the architecture name in `SparseCoderConfig` before training begins. The next coherent move is a narrow code-fix round that wires `factorized_topk` into the existing config/class registry and forward path so the prototype can finally receive one real K=128 proxy measurement under the strongest current regime.

jumprelu.design_hypothesis: Returning to the best known working JumpReLU K=128 configuration and modestly increasing AUXK_ALPHA to 0.0625 may improve training stability and feature health without changing the core architecture or optimization regime, potentially recovering some proxy quality after the recent stalled sweeps.
known_issues: round 3: suspected performance regression for jumprelu (tps=16601.650675, baseline_ratio=0.200000, termination=throughput_too_low)
next_steps: If AUXK_ALPHA=0.0625 improves proxy FVU, promote to full and treat the higher-auxk regime as the new stable JumpReLU anchor.
If AUXK_ALPHA=0.0625 is worse but training remains healthy, test LR=2e-4 on the same restored baseline as the next stability-oriented adjustment.
If stability problems persist even on the restored baseline, pause architecture exploration and continue only with conservative recovery steps around the known-good JumpReLU configuration.

gated.design_hypothesis: The lower-LR gated sweep was a clean regression, so the next narrow exploitation move is to test the remaining untried capacity point on the best stable family. Reducing gated `EXPANSION_FACTOR` from 16 to 4 at K=128 may reveal a non-dominated quality-cost tradeoff and will close the last obvious one-parameter sweep on the current anchor before returning to broader architecture work.
next_steps: If `EXPANSION_FACTOR=4` is unexpectedly competitive, keep it alive as a quality-cost tradeoff candidate and consider checking runtime or memory benefits before discarding it.
If `EXPANSION_FACTOR=4` is clearly worse, conclude that the obvious gated one-parameter sweep is exhausted and resume architecture exploration or fix the blocked `factorized_topk` sanity issue.
If `EXPANSION_FACTOR=4` materially improves proxy FVU, promote it to full and treat lower-width gated training as the new mainline stabilization candidate.

topk.design_hypothesis: After multiple rounds dominated by gated-local tuning and unresolved crash recovery, the most informative next move is to advance an already-incubating alternate family rather than spend another round near the current gated anchor. A clean `topk` K=128 proxy run under the current strongest gated training regime tests whether simpler support selection benefits more from the improved optimizer and regularization settings than it did in earlier comparisons.
next_steps: If `topk` improves materially over its prior proxy results and is competitive with gated at K=128, promote it to full and begin family-specific stabilization from this stronger anchor.
If `topk` runs normally but remains clearly worse than gated, retire it from near-term focus and move to a more expressive new architecture family.
If `topk` is still worse on FVU but shows meaningfully better runtime or memory behavior, keep it alive as a cost-quality tradeoff family rather than a main quality contender.

routed.design_hypothesis: Seven non-improving rounds indicate the current gated-centered search is fundamentally stalled, and the updated routed encoder is now the highest-value different architecture to test because it separates support selection from reconstruction magnitude without relying on the same gated monotone ranking. A clean proxy run of routed at K=128 under the best validated training regime can reveal whether this support-selection change opens a new quality path that top-k and gated did not.
next_steps: If routed proxy FVU is competitive with or better than top-k and gated, keep the family alive and promote or stabilize it as the next main architecture path.
If routed runs cleanly but is clearly worse, conclude that this support-selection variant is not the missing ingredient and deprioritize it.
If routed quality is promising but throughput regresses, follow with a routed-specific performance pass rather than discarding the family on speed alone.

next_steps: If the patched `group_topk` run completes and is competitive with gated at K=128, keep the family alive and start stabilizing its grouped-selection design inside `sparsify/`.
If the run completes normally but `group_topk` is still clearly worse on FVU, retire hard grouped competition and move to a more expressive new family rather than more selector variants.
If `group_topk` still hits a first-step timeout after this shared trainer fix, stop spending rounds on it and treat the remaining issue as a deeper execution-path incompatibility.
